Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Endocrinology digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 21 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Endocrinology.

21articles that week
5journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Diabetes care

The Importance of C-Peptide for Developing T1D Disease-Modifying Therapies

Fleming GA, Mathieu C, Skyler JS · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Commentary
Key Question: Should C-peptide be reinstated as a regulatory endpoint for full approval of type 1 diabetes disease-modifying therapies?
Key Findings:
  • Preserved β-cell function (measured by C-peptide) correlates with better glycaemic control, fewer microvascular complications, and reduced hypoglycaemia risk in T1D
  • The FDA accepted C-peptide as a primary efficacy endpoint from 2008-2023 but subsequently removed it from the Surrogate Endpoint Table for unclear reasons
  • Current regulatory pathways requiring clinical endpoints create lengthy approval processes that may delay access to potentially beneficial therapies
Clinical Relevance: This advocacy piece addresses a key barrier to developing new T1D treatments that could benefit patients managed in NHS diabetes services by potentially accelerating regulatory approval timelines.
Limitations: This is an opinion piece rather than original research, providing advocacy arguments without new empirical evidence.
PubMed →DOI →
Diabetes care

Integrating Alcohol Use Into MASLD Care: A Practical Guide for Diabetes and Obesity Care Teams

Alexopoulos AS, Cusi K · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Clinical review/practical guide
Key Question: How should diabetes and obesity care teams integrate alcohol assessment and management into care pathways for patients with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD)?
Key Findings:
  • Even light-to-moderate alcohol intake accelerates MASLD progression to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma in patients with metabolic disease
  • MetALD (metabolic dysfunction- and alcohol-associated liver disease) carries higher all-cause, cancer-related, and liver-related mortality compared to MASLD alone
  • Diagnostic workup should include AUDIT questionnaires and phosphatidylethanol testing to stratify alcohol use alongside standard MASLD assessment
Clinical Relevance: Given MASLD affects most patients with diabetes and severe obesity seen in UK endocrinology services, systematic alcohol assessment could identify higher-risk patients requiring intensified hepatic monitoring and intervention.
Limitations: Treatment evidence for MetALD specifically remains limited compared to MASLD alone.
PubMed →DOI →
Diabetes care

Insulin Hypersecretion and Increased Ectopic Fat in South Asian American Adolescents and Young Adults Compared With White and African American Peers: The CHARISMA Study

Hitt TA, Kelly A, Stefanovski D et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Cross-sectional comparative study
Key Question: How do metabolic mechanisms underlying diabetes risk differ between South Asian, White, and African American adolescents and young adults?
Key Findings:
  • South Asian participants (n=53) had significantly higher visceral fat and liver fat compared to White (n=53) and African American (n=49) peers of similar BMI
  • South Asian individuals showed lower insulin sensitivity (P=0.006 vs White) and higher insulin secretion rates (P<0.001 vs both groups), with lower insulin clearance
  • When visceral or liver fat was controlled for, differences in insulin sensitivity and dynamic insulin secretion lost significance, but elevated total insulin secretion persisted in South Asian participants
Clinical Relevance: This provides mechanistic insight into early diabetes risk in young South Asians, relevant for UK endocrinology services given the substantial South Asian population and their disproportionate diabetes burden.
Limitations: Cross-sectional design prevents determination of causality or prediction of future diabetes development.
PubMed →DOI →
Diabetes care

Long-term Air Pollution and Overall and Regional Body Composition in Older Adults With Overweight or Obesity and Metabolic Syndrome

Curto A, Konieczna J, Colom A et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Prospective cohort study (secondary analysis of RCT data)
Key Question: Does long-term air pollution exposure affect body composition in older adults with overweight/obesity and metabolic syndrome?
Key Findings:
  • Black carbon exposure associated with 1.01% higher body fat percentage (95% CI 0.31-1.71) and 0.74 kg lower lean mass (95% CI -1.37 to -0.12) at baseline
  • Similar adverse associations observed with PM2.5 and NO2 for body fat and lean mass percentages
  • Effects on visceral fat mass limited to participants under 65 years; associations persisted over 3-year follow-up
Clinical Relevance: Air pollution may exacerbate sarcopenic obesity in metabolically vulnerable older adults, potentially worsening diabetes and cardiovascular risk profiles in UK patients with similar demographic characteristics.
Limitations: Observational design cannot establish causality despite longitudinal follow-up.
PubMed →DOI →
Diabetes care

Life Course Cumulative BMI Burden From Childhood to Adulthood and Risk of Metabolic Multimorbidity: A 36-Year Prospective Cohort Study

Guo T, Li H, Liu Z et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Prospective cohort study
Key Question: Does cumulative BMI burden from childhood through adulthood increase the risk of developing multiple metabolic diseases in adulthood?
Key Findings:
  • Higher cumulative BMI exposure across the life course significantly increased odds of metabolic multimorbidity (≥2 conditions), with ORs ranging 1.51-2.59 for total exposure and 1.94-4.33 for incremental exposure
  • Rapid BMI gain in childhood showed stronger associations with multimorbidity (OR 4.33) than absolute BMI levels (OR 1.51), while sustained high BMI in adulthood showed the opposite pattern
  • Associations were significantly stronger in males than females for adult and life-course BMI burden
Clinical Relevance: This 36-year follow-up study provides evidence for targeted obesity prevention strategies across different life stages, informing NHS childhood obesity programmes and adult weight management pathways.
Limitations: Single-centre Chinese cohort may limit generalisability to UK populations with different genetic and environmental risk profiles.
PubMed →DOI →
Diabetes care

The Effectiveness of Diabetes Prevention Programs Delivered in Primary Care for Individuals With Prediabetes or at Risk of Developing Type 2 Diabetes: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis

Qiang J, Pathmaraj M, Lo T et al. · 2026 Apr 24
Study Type: Systematic review and meta-analysis
Key Question: Are lifestyle interventions delivered in primary care effective at preventing type 2 diabetes in patients with prediabetes or high diabetes risk?
Key Findings:
  • No significant reduction in diabetes incidence (RR 0.82, 95% CI 0.65-1.02) across 14 studies
  • No significant improvements in HbA1c (MD -0.41%, 95% CI -0.94 to 0.12), glucose levels, weight, BMI, or waist circumference
  • High heterogeneity (>50%) was identified, with one study driving much of the variation between results
Clinical Relevance: Challenges current assumptions about primary care-based diabetes prevention programmes, suggesting UK practices may need more integrated approaches beyond standard lifestyle advice for high-risk patients.
Limitations: English-language studies only may limit generalisability to diverse UK populations.
PubMed →DOI →
Endocrine reviews

Glycated CD59, a Novel Biomarker for Gestational Diabetes

Toth Castillo M, Drayton Powell S, Biermann A et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Review article
Key Question: Can plasma glycated CD59 (pGCD59) serve as an alternative biomarker to oral glucose tolerance tests for screening and diagnosing gestational diabetes mellitus?
Key Findings:
  • Authors developed pGCD59 as a biomarker based on glucose modification of complement inhibitor CD59
  • Clinical validation conducted across six human studies (specific performance metrics not provided in abstract)
  • Proposed as alternative to OGTT for GDM screening, diagnosis, and monitoring
Clinical Relevance: This could address a significant NHS clinical need, as current OGTT screening (24-28 weeks) has poor patient tolerance and practical implementation challenges, potentially improving GDM detection rates and maternal-fetal outcomes.
Limitations: Abstract lacks essential performance data including sensitivity, specificity, or comparison with OGTT diagnostic accuracy.
PubMed →DOI →
Endocrine reviews

Diverse SOX3 genetic variants and their associated phenotypic spectrum in human disease

De Dominicis C, Birtolo MF, Lania AG et al. · 2026 Apr 24
Study Type: Systematic review
Key Question: What is the spectrum of SOX3 genetic variants and their associated clinical phenotypes in human disease?
Key Findings:
  • SOX3 variants include single nucleotide variants, indels, polyalanine tract changes, copy number variants, and structural rearrangements
  • Associated phenotypes range from pan-hypopituitarism and isolated growth hormone deficiency to neural tube defects and disorders of sex development
  • Different pathogenetic mechanisms are involved, including gene dosage effects and altered transcriptional regulation
Clinical Relevance: This provides UK endocrinologists with a comprehensive framework for interpreting SOX3 variants when investigating patients with hypopituitarism, growth hormone deficiency, or complex developmental disorders involving the hypothalamic-pituitary axis.
Limitations: As a review paper, findings are dependent on the quality and completeness of previously published case reports and studies.
PubMed →DOI →
